nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered rocky and coral reefs; benthic dweller typically found among sponges, algae, and rubble at shallow depths

diet.notes: Ambush predators that lure prey using a modified first dorsal spine (illicium) tipped with a fleshy lure (esca). Generally feed on small fish and crustaceans, swallowing prey whole. Newly introduced specimens typically require live saltwater feeder shrimp to initiate a feeding response; many individuals can gradually be trained to accept frozen meaty foods such as silversides or krill offered from tongs or a feeding stick. Feeding every 3–5 days is generally advised, as overfeeding has been reported to lead to prolonged food refusal.

goodWith: large peaceful fish that cannot fit in the angler's mouth
non-aggressive fish of similar or larger size
avoidWith: fish small enough to be swallowed whole

Rickard Zerpe; 3911x2933. Antennarius pictus (painted frogfish): striking pink-red individual with dark ocellated spots, sharp, whole body against dark substrate.
Bernard DUPONT; 4416x2649. Antennarius pictus in bright orange-red juvenile colour on black sand; sharp, whole body, different morph to the pink one.
Diego Delso; 7060x4707. Antennarius maculatus in pale cream-yellow with red-margined patches; very high resolution, sharp, whole body; second maculatus colour form.
Rickard Zerpe; 3456x2592. Antennarius striatus (striped/hairy frogfish) in golden colour showing the diagnostic hair-like dermal spinules; sharp, whole body.
Austen Novis; ~1024x768. Antennarius multiocellatus (longlure frogfish) in vivid yellow-green with dark ocelli on a reef; sharp, whole body; adds a distinct Atlantic species.
Vassil; 3644x2646. Antennarius sp. (genus-level ID) photographed in an aquarium on live rock; sharp, whole body in profile, shows the fish as hobbyists see it.

summary: The Assorted Angler is a trade label for several Antennarius species — camouflaged ambush predators that lure prey with a modified spine. Generally recommended for intermediate keepers; tends to require live prey at first and will consume any tankmate small enough to swallow.
careNotes:
The Assorted Angler listing covers multiple species within the genus Antennarius (family Antennariidae, order Lophiiformes), sold in a range of color morphs including black, tan, and yellow. The scientific name is listed as Antennarius sp. rather than a single species because the specimens on offer may include Antennarius pictus (Painted Frogfish, up to 30 cm), Antennarius hispidus (Shaggy Angler, up to 20 cm), Antennarius maculatus (Wartskin Frogfish, up to 15 cm), or other mid-sized members of the genus. Aquarists should be aware that the adult size and care requirements of the specimen they receive may vary depending on the actual species.

All Antennarius tend to be benthic ambush predators — bottom-dwellers that remain nearly motionless for extended periods — making them well-suited to aquariums with ample live rock, sponges, and caves for perching. They have a remarkable ability to change color over days to weeks to match their surroundings. The fish hunts by wiggling the illicium (modified dorsal spine) tipped with a fleshy lure (esca) resembling a small fish or shrimp, then lunging forward with an extremely rapid strike to vacuum prey into its highly expandable mouth. Individuals have been documented consuming prey close to their own body size, so any small fish or motile invertebrate sharing the tank should be considered at risk.

Feeding difficulty is the principal husbandry challenge: freshly imported specimens commonly refuse prepared or frozen foods, and starvation is a recognized risk if a transition to non-live foods is not achieved. Many keepers report success training the fish onto frozen silversides or krill by mimicking prey movement with tongs. A tightly fitting lid is advisable, as individuals of several Antennarius species have occasionally been reported leaving the water.
breedingNotes: Antennarius species are egg-layers (oviparous): the female releases eggs enclosed in a ribbon-like or veil-like gelatinous raft that drifts in the water column. Captive breeding has been documented for some species within the genus but is generally considered uncommon. Because this trade listing may represent several different species, the identity of the actual specimen will affect breeding feasibility and pairing compatibility.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry data — scientific name listed as Antennarius sp., size (up to 9"), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), tank size (30 gal), care level (moderate), diet, temperament, reef compatibility.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked genus coverage (12 Antennarius species), size range (6.3–45 cm across species), temperature ranges for commonly traded species: A. pictus 25.1–29.3°C, A. hispidus 24.6–29.1°C. Temperature discrepancy noted with LiveAquaria (22.2–25.6°C vs FishBase 24.6–29.3°C); averaged to 23.5–27.5°C. Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; BlueZoo Aquatics returned an unrelated product page; Fishkeeping World and TFH Magazine both returned 404. FishBase used as sole secondary; minimum-confirmation bar met with primary + FishBase.

fish.breedingNotes: Antennarius species are egg-layers: eggs are released encased in a gelatinous ribbon or veil that floats in the water column. Captive breeding has been documented for some species in the genus but is generally considered uncommon, and the species identity of the specific specimen will affect breeding feasibility.
